video_title: Hotel Investing at EOS – Jonathan Wang
gist: Jonathan Wang, founder of EOS Investors ($2B AUM), lays out a data-driven framework for hotel investing that weights market selection at 75%+ of the investment decision, uses 40 years of demand/supply data to identify occupancy-driven pricing power thresholds, and explains how vertical integration of ownership and operations creates durable alpha. The conversation covers his expansion into residential and hotel credit as complementary platforms, and flags two emerging structural breaks — a 2025 decline in hotel demand that snapped a 40-year streak, and the US flipping from a net importer to net exporter of travel.

insights.claim: Hotel room demand across the US has grown at approximately 2% per year since 1987 — a 40-year trend that has only broken during global shocks (Gulf War 1991, 9/11 2001, GFC 2008-09, COVID 2020). This stability underpins EOS's scientific underwriting approach.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: Investors who price in high volatility for hotels are mispricing the asset class; the demand trend is far more stable than perceived, creating opportunity for those who trust the data.
insights.quote: if you go back all the way to 1987, which is as far back as you have data, you can see that demand for hotel rooms across the US has grown at just about 2% a year for 40 years and only ever declined in periods of global shock

insights.claim: In 2025, US hotel demand declined outside of a recognized global shock event — snapping a 40-year unbroken rule. Wang is watching closely to determine if Liberation Day tariff volatility constitutes a new category of demand disruptor or signals a structural break.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: The single most important pillar of EOS's stability thesis was violated in 2025; investors should reassess whether the demand baseline is still reliable as the macro shock taxonomy expands.
insights.quote: I started off this by saying since 1987, you've only had hotel demand decrease in years of large global shock. What I left out was that it declined last year. Now, you could say, was Liberation Day global shock? Stock markets up. I don't think so. That broke a 40-year rule.

insights.claim: Every hotel market and submarket has a specific occupancy threshold — generally around 72% — below which there is effectively zero pricing power, and above which operators can push rate by approximately 5% on average. This threshold differs by market based on seasonality and existing supply levels.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: Underwriting RevPAR growth requires first solving for whether a market will cross its occupancy inflection point; buying below the threshold locks in zero pricing leverage regardless of asset quality.
insights.quote: below 72% occupancy, you have no pricing power. And above 72% occupancy, you might be able to push rate on average 5%

insights.claim: Hotel supply is visible 2-3 years out in the pipeline, giving investors enough foresight to model occupancy trajectories. Combined with the stable 2% demand trend, this allows construction of what Wang calls 'compression charts' that map occupancy to pricing power with high reliability over medium-term horizons.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: Hotels are more forecastable than widely believed; investors who build supply/demand models using public pipeline data can underwrite RevPAR with a scientific rigor not commonly applied to the sector.

insights.claim: Wang assigns 75% or more of investment return attribution to getting the market right, with only the remainder driven by idiosyncratic asset-level execution. Most hotel investors skew this weighting the other way, over-indexing on business plan creativity.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: Selecting the wrong market cannot be overcome by stellar asset-level execution; the primary due diligence leverage point is market analysis, not property-specific underwriting.
insights.quote: if I had to bifurcate the market underwriting from the idiosyncratic asset underwriting, I would give a 75% weight to getting the market right, if not higher

insights.claim: The traditional hotel PE model — institutional capital partnering with third-party operators who sourced deals — misaligns incentives by ceding sourcing control and giving away upside. EOS's solution was to become both owner and operator (vertically integrated), capturing the full economics and controlling the entire investment funnel.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: Vertical integration in hotels is a structural competitive moat: EOS sees deals before the market, underwrites with operational credibility, and captures management economics that otherwise leak to third parties.
insights.quote: what if we're both sides of it? What if we're the operator and we're the owner and we create this vertical integration?

insights.claim: EOS's owned hotel management company oversees ~60 properties — 45 owned by EOS funds and ~15 for external sophisticated owners — creating a scaled platform that generates data, talent, and operational capability advantages that compound over time.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: At 60 managed properties EOS has enough critical mass to attract top hospitality talent and generate proprietary operational benchmarks unavailable to smaller or non-integrated investors.

insights.claim: The operational and acquisition teams at EOS are treated as equal partners throughout the investment lifecycle — acquisitions leads before purchase, operations leads after — with full transparency on underwriting assumptions and shared accountability. Misalignment between these teams is a primary source of value destruction in typical PE hotel investing.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: Hotel PE funds that hand off from deal team to ops team post-close create a principal-agent problem where operators inherit unrealistic proformas; shared underwriting from the start eliminates this.
insights.quote: The real unlocking of vertical integration comes when the operations team and the investment teams work as one.

insights.claim: Business plan execution falls into two buckets: physical capital improvements (ranging from $5M to $70M for renovations, lobby redesigns, amenity additions, room repositioning) and operational changes (revenue strategy, cost reduction, or strategic service expansion). EOS begins executing on day one of ownership.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: The size of the capital plan and the split between physical vs. operational levers materially affects risk and return timing; operational-only plans deliver faster cash-on-cash, while major capex plans require longer hold periods.

insights.claim: EOS treats design, food & beverage quality, and experiential excellence as upside optionality — not base case inputs. The base case return must be achievable with professional-grade execution; outperformance from exceptional design or F&B is layered on top.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: Hotel funds that bake best-in-class F&B or design outcomes into their base case underwriting are taking on uncompensated risk; conservatively underwriting creative upside is the disciplined approach.
insights.quote: We always want to do that, but we want that to be our upside... If you do a fabulous job, you should make a great return. If you do a good job, you should make your base case.

insights.claim: EOS concentrates 90% of hotel investments in seven large urban markets (Boston, New York, DC, Miami, Chicago, San Francisco, LA) plus coastal resort markets along the East and West coasts. They have never invested in Hawaii despite consistently evaluating it.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: Geographic concentration in deep, liquid markets with long data histories allows more reliable supply/demand modeling; gateway cities provide multiple demand drivers and exit liquidity unavailable in secondary markets.

insights.claim: New York City was the best-performing hotel market globally from 1987-2007, then became the worst-performing US hotel market from 2007-2019 — entirely driven by supply dynamics, not demand destruction. This illustrates how a top-market can become a value trap when supply overshoots.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: Market-level supply pipeline analysis must be the first screen, even in historically dominant markets; NYC's two-decade swing demonstrates that brand-name markets carry significant supply risk.
insights.quote: If you go back the most interesting and best performing hotel market in the world from 1987 to 2007 was New York City. The worst performing hotel market in the United States from 2007 to 2019 was New York City. That's largely because of the way supply came into that market over that decade.

insights.claim: EOS has been the most active investor in the Florida Keys since 2011, buying and selling 17 hotels there over 15 years. Deep market familiarity enabled them to underwrite opportunistic COVID-era acquisitions with high conviction when others couldn't.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: Returning to the same markets repeatedly builds a proprietary information edge — long-term relationships with sellers, operators, and lenders generate deal flow unavailable to occasional market entrants.

insights.claim: EOS discovered in 2019 that the drive-to leisure resort dynamic they had identified in Florida and California also existed in other coastal markets within a 3-hour drive. This insight unlocked Cape Cod, Kennebunkport (Maine), Delaware Beaches, and Hilton Head as investable markets where EOS is now one of the largest property owners.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: Regional drive-to coastal resorts follow the same supply/demand dynamics as primary resort markets but trade at discounts due to perceived secondary status; early movers in these markets capture outsized returns.
insights.quote: It wasn't until 2019 when we bought a big great property in Myrtle Beach, South Carolina that we realized that the same dynamics exist in other coastal markets that are within 3-hour drive-to.

insights.claim: EOS deliberately avoids high-supply markets like Austin and Nashville even when demand keeps pace, because they believe supply overhang eventually dominates. They acknowledge missing returns in those markets for over 15 years but view the discipline as necessary risk management.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: Supply-discipline filters out high-momentum markets that look attractive on trailing performance but carry significant mean-reversion risk when supply catches up to demand.
insights.quote: markets like Austin, markets like Nashville have been great performers for people over a large period of the last 15 years. If we're not early into those markets, we let that opportunity pass.

insights.claim: EOS's four investment pillars are: (1) interesting supply/demand dynamics over the short-to-medium term, (2) non-commoditized assets that guests will pay a premium for rather than booking on lowest-price channels, (3) diversified demand drivers across group, business transient, and leisure segments, and (4) conservative leverage to avoid being forced into bad decisions during volatility.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: These four filters in combination create downside protection (diversified demand + conservative leverage) while targeting asymmetric upside (non-commodity + favorable supply/demand); the combination is more defensive than typical hotel PE.

insights.claim: Non-commodity hotel assets include oceanfront resorts, standalone villa properties, and urban hotels with strong leisure weekend demand in addition to business transient. The test is whether guests will pay $10 more to stay there versus a comparable nearby option — driven by location, physical layout, or amenity base.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: Non-commoditized positioning is the key RevPAR resilience factor; hotels that compete purely on channel-driven price discovery are structurally unable to hold rate during soft demand periods.

insights.claim: Conservative leverage is not primarily about return optimization but about preserving optionality during short-term shocks. Hotels have no future revenue commitments (daily leases), so leverage that is serviceable in steady-state can become existential during demand disruptions of even a few months.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: In hotel investing, leverage sizing should be stress-tested against a scenario where revenue drops to zero for multiple months — a realistic tail risk as COVID demonstrated; most hotel financing underweights this.
insights.quote: It's tough to be scientific when you have daily leases and no long-term commitments.

insights.claim: When Florida lifted COVID travel restrictions and capped hotel occupancy at 50%, EOS properties in the Florida Keys filled to the cap at rates higher than pre-COVID levels. This same-day demand signal gave EOS high conviction to aggressively pursue acquisitions while the broader hotel market was paralyzed.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: Operators with pre-existing assets in a market gain real-time demand intelligence unavailable to outside investors; this proprietary data advantage is most valuable during dislocations when it translates directly into conviction to transact.
insights.quote: we could fill up to max occupancy at rates that were higher than they were before the world shut down. Basically, the next day, we had this unbelievable data about how demand was going to be for leisure assets

insights.claim: A Florida Keys developer with a full-recourse construction loan was forced to sell during COVID at a price EOS immediately recognized as highly attractive. EOS's 10-year market presence and existing lender relationship enabled them to be called directly, bypassing any competitive process.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: Full-recourse lenders in distressed situations are highly motivated sellers who call known buyers first; building long-term lender relationships in target markets generates first-look deal access during dislocations.

insights.claim: On the Florida Keys marina hotel acquisition, EOS's value creation levers included: removing the brand (cutting brand fees while enabling revenue flexibility), condominiumizing the marina for separate sale to an emerging marina roll-up buyer, and spending ~$1M on landscaping to visually separate the property from the road. The marina was sold to a roll-up group; the hotel was sold to a local owner.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: Segmenting mixed-use hotel/marina assets unlocks separate buyer pools (institutional roll-ups for marina, local operators for hotel) and can generate total value that exceeds what a single buyer would pay for the combined asset.

insights.claim: EOS was almost entirely invested in drive-to leisure resort assets when COVID hit — a concentration that converted a catastrophic industry shock into an outperformance event. The positioning was not a COVID bet but rather a pre-existing thesis about domestic travel resilience and non-air-travel leisure demand.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: Thesis-driven geographic concentration that happens to be defensive during shocks creates compounding advantages: not only do returns hold up, but talent and deal flow gravitate toward the perceived winner.
insights.quote: CO was totally unexpected yet we found ourselves 90% allocated into a drive to leisure thesis which was the small subsector of hospitality that took off like a rocket right in the middle of co

insights.claim: The US flipped from a net importer of travel (more inbound foreign visitors staying in US hotels than outbound US travelers) pre-2019 to a significant net exporter of travel by 2025-2026. This dual headwind — fewer inbound visitors, more US travelers going abroad — is structurally negative for US hotel RevPAR and largely underdiscussed in the industry.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: US hotels dependent on inbound international visitors face a structural demand headwind that is not merely cyclical; underwriters using pre-2019 demand comps for urban markets with high foreign visitor exposure are using stale baselines.
insights.quote: up until 2019, the US was an importer of travel. More people came to the US and stayed in hotels than left the US to stay abroad. We have now become a significant exporter of travel.

insights.claim: Business transient travel has structurally declined post-COVID due to the permanent adoption of video conferencing, and Wang identifies this as a trend that was already building pre-COVID — the pandemic merely accelerated adoption and locked in behavioral change.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: Hotels with high business transient exposure (particularly urban convention/corporate hotels) face a structural, not cyclical, reduction in their highest-margin demand segment; leisure/resort assets are structurally better positioned.
insights.quote: business travel is down because a lot of people are doing Zoom meetings. That wasn't something that just happened overnight. The adoption was accelerated because we were all forced to do it so quickly and it's held.

insights.claim: Younger generations' preference for spending on experiences over material goods — combined with the potential AI-driven desire for more human interaction as automation increases — creates multi-decade tailwinds for leisure travel demand.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: Demographic spending shifts toward experiences support the leisure hotel thesis regardless of near-term macro conditions; the AI-driven human connection thesis is more speculative but directionally consistent with remote work isolation trends.
insights.quote: the younger generations want to spend more money on experiences and travel than they do on material goods

insights.claim: Domestic travel has been more structurally resilient than international travel over the past 20 years due to increasing difficulty of international travel (visa friction, flight costs, geopolitical complexity), and EOS systematically invests in domestic travel assets because of this relative stability.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: The domestic vs. international travel bifurcation is a long-term secular trend, not a COVID anomaly; hotel investors overweighting US inbound international demand exposure are betting against a 20-year trend.
insights.quote: International travel has generally become harder over the last 20 years. Whether it's visa issues or cost of flights... we generally are investing in domestic travel because it's more stable.

insights.claim: EOS launched a residential real estate investment platform approximately 3 years ago in partnership with Nicole Cerier, whose track record distinguishes itself by actively pivoting across residential subsectors (multifamily, senior housing, etc.) based on risk/reward, rather than riding one subsector through a full cycle. Senior housing was EOS's highest-conviction residential trade over the past 2+ years.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: Senior housing was identified as the most attractive risk/reward investment across both hotel and residential for 2023-2025; the ability to compare opportunities across sectors using a common risk/reward framework is a genuine cross-platform advantage.

insights.claim: EOS launched a hotel credit platform in the past year, led by Christopher Jordan — Wells Fargo's former head of hotel/hospitality lending, described as the most prolific hotel lender over the past two decades. The credit strategy targets a gap created by bank regulatory tightening and the broader private credit expansion in real estate.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: Combining EOS's ownership/operational intelligence with best-in-class hotel lending expertise creates an underwriting edge in hotel credit that pure credit shops lack; the bank regulatory retreat creates persistent structural supply of lending opportunities.

insights.claim: Wang observed that credit returns became more attractive than equity returns when interest rates rose sharply in 2022, and has been tracking the hotel credit opportunity for nearly four years before launching the platform. The decision to wait for the right talent (Jordan) rather than entering the market early reflects a stated principle of only launching platforms with the best available operator.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: Timing a credit platform launch around the availability of a specific marquee talent, rather than purely on market opportunity, is a disciplined approach that avoids the common mistake of building a credit team with B-tier personnel just to capture a favorable rate environment.

insights.claim: EOS will only launch a new investment platform if it meets four criteria: (1) decades-long opportunity tailwinds, (2) a unique top-tier talent partner available, (3) realistic path to being best-in-class, and (4) the new platform makes existing platforms better. The residential business made the hotel business better by providing a cross-asset comparison that prevented tunnel-vision hotel deal-making.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: This four-filter platform launch criterion prevents AUM-driven diversification that destroys value; the 'must make other platforms better' filter is particularly valuable as it creates genuine cross-pollination rather than siloed diversification.
insights.quote: We will never set up a platform that doesn't do four things. One is an opportunity set that we think has decades of tailwinds behind it. Two, we don't have a unique talent... Third, we should never do it unless we think we can be the best or close to the best. Fourth, we won't do it unless it makes our other businesses better.

themes.theme: Market Timing, Patience & Current Opportunity Set

insights.claim: In 6 of 9 years since EOS's 2017 founding, the firm made zero or one investments. Discipline around not investing is a structural feature of the strategy, not a failure mode — EOS stays fully staffed and active during low-investment periods.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: Hotel markets with attractive entry conditions represent a minority of years; a framework-disciplined investor must be structurally prepared to be out of the market for extended periods without the GP pressure to deploy that afflicts fund-cycle-constrained vehicles.
insights.quote: in six of the nine years that we've been around, we've made one or zero investments. When we have deep conviction, we move fast and we'll invest pretty heavily.

insights.claim: Wang sees the current environment (2026) as potentially entering a highly active acquisition phase, driven by: banks forcing sellers to resolve problem loans, seller capitulation on pricing, unprecedented divergence between market and submarket performance levels that makes comparable sales useless, and widening dispersion creating mispricing.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: Distressed supply is beginning to flow into the market as lenders and sellers exhaust their ability to extend and pretend; acquirers with operational credibility and sector expertise are positioned to capture opportunistic deals at significant discounts.
insights.quote: We're finally starting to see, which is exciting, is that banks are starting to make people deal with problems or sellers are now capitulating a bit. There's going to be a much more active investment environment over the next couple years.

insights.claim: Wang describes the current market as the most difficult environment he has seen to analyze due to unprecedented performance divergence — markets and submarkets moving in dramatically different directions simultaneously, making comparable-sales-based underwriting unreliable. This complexity itself creates opportunity for analytical investors.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: Periods of maximum analytical complexity create the widest information gaps between disciplined framework-based investors and peers relying on comps; this is when the EOS supply/demand model creates the most alpha.
insights.quote: I have never in my career seen assets and markets and types of travel perform in a less coordinated way... it's never been harder to comp to the like sale.

insights.claim: Wang founded EOS in 2017 deliberately at a late point in the cycle, believing you cannot pick your entry timing — only control your pace of deployment. He raised limited capital so the portfolio was not pressured to deploy, positioning EOS to survive to the next attractive environment.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wang
insights.so_what: Starting a hotel fund late-cycle with modest initial capital and controlled deployment pacing is structurally superior to large fund launches that pressure GPs to invest regardless of market conditions.
insights.quote: you can't pick your timing. You can just figure out your pace of deployment.
